# Fixture: cns-graphical-abstract

## Task
Produce a graphical abstract for a Cell-tier submission. The paper introduces RetroDiff, a retrieval-augmented diffusion method for chemical generation. Output a single landscape figure (figure.pdf, ~1200×600 px equivalent / 2:1 aspect) suitable for the journal table-of-contents listing.

The contribution should be conveyed in ≤ 5 visual elements. Reader should grasp it in < 5 seconds without consulting the paper body.

## Inputs
- claim.json: the system name, three claims, retrieval mechanism, target chemistry. Use this as the scientific content.
- captions.json: not used for this fixture.

## Outputs (cwd)
- figure.pdf, figure.png   (the graphical abstract; landscape 2:1 aspect)
- caption.tex              (one-line graphical-abstract caption — usually a take-home statement)
- gen_figure.py            (the generation script — matplotlib + tikz + pdflatex acceptable; AI-generation route also acceptable but include a stub script that can re-render from a saved prompt)

## Hints
- Pattern: pick "workflow" / "mechanism" / "headline result" — see graphical-abstract.md guidance if your loaded skills include cns-paper-discipline.
- ≤ 4 hues, ColorBrewer or hand-picked. White background.
- Self-contained: no "see Fig. 3" cross-references.
- Font ≥ 9 pt at final size.
- One headline-finding sentence (≤ 12 words) embedded in the figure.
- This figure is graded against CNS journal expectations (Cell, Nat Commun, Sci Adv).

content.content:
1	# Graphical abstract
2	
3	Provenance: scientific-writing-kdense ("Every scientific writeup MUST include a graphical abstract"). Mandatory in Cell, Cell Reports, iScience, Cell Press journals; recommended (and increasingly enforced) in Nature, Science, NEJM.
4	
5	A graphical abstract is a single visual summary of the paper. It appears before/after the text abstract on the journal page and in the table-of-contents listing. Its job is to make a busy reader who scans the TOC stop and read the paper.
6	
7	## Hard requirements
8	
9	- **Aspect ratio**: landscape, ~2:1 (Cell prefers 1200×600 px or 5×2.5 inches at 300 dpi). Some Nature sub-journals want square (1:1).
10	- **Resolution**: 300 dpi at final size for raster; vector PDF preferred when text is present.
11	- **No more than 5–7 visual elements** in the figure. The reader processes it in < 5 seconds.
12	- **Self-contained**: must be readable WITHOUT the paper. No "see Fig. 3" cross-references. Define every label inside the abstract figure.
13	- **Color discipline**: ≤ 4 hues, ColorBrewer or hand-picked. No rainbow.
14	- **Font**: same family as the body figures; ≥ 9 pt at final size.
15	- **No text-only**: a graphical abstract that is just a paragraph in a box is not a graphical abstract.
16	
17	## Content patterns (pick one)
18	
19	Three patterns cover ≈ 80% of CNS graphical abstracts. Pick the one that matches the paper's contribution.
20	
21	### Pattern 1 — Workflow / pipeline
22	
23	Left-to-right or top-to-bottom flow: input → method → output. Use when the paper's contribution is methodological.
24	
25	```
26	[Sample] → [Method box (named)] → [Result figure miniature] → [Headline finding text]
27	```
28	
29	Implementation: [[figure-spec]] archetype A (boxes-and-arrows), or [[hero-figure-prompt]] for a richer illustrated version.
30	
31	### Pattern 2 — Mechanism / model
32	
33	A cartoon of the biological / physical / computational mechanism the paper proposes. Used when the contribution is a *new understanding* rather than a new method.
34	
35	```
36	[Before state diagram] → [Mechanism arrow with key molecule / equation] → [After state diagram]
37	```
38	
39	Implementation: usually [[hero-figure-prompt]] (gpt-image-2.0 with the persistent prompt file). LaTeX TikZ as fallback.
40	
41	### Pattern 3 — Headline result
42	
43	A single "hero plot" — the strongest single panel from the paper, blown up and cleaned. Used when the contribution is a *quantitative finding*.
44	
45	```
46	[Hero plot, e.g. dose-response or scaling-law] + [one-line take-home in bold]
47	```
48	
49	Implementation: [[academic-plotting]] for the plot + [[hero-figure-prompt]] for the title overlay.
50	
51	## Generation routes
52	
53	### Route A — gpt-image-2.0 via hero-figure-prompt
54	
55	For mechanism / pipeline / illustrated abstracts. Always go through [[hero-figure-prompt]] so the prompt is persisted as `branches/writer/paper/figures/graphical_abstract.prompt.md`. Regeneration must be possible at camera-ready time.
56	
57	Baseline prompt skeleton (drop into `hero-figure-prompt`):
58	
59	```
60	Generate a graphical abstract for a Cell Press submission.
61	Aspect: landscape 2:1 (1200×600 px).
62	Style: flat vector illustration, clean lines, scientific aesthetic.
63	Palette: 3-4 colors, professional pastel + one accent. White background.
64	Layout: <left/right OR top/bottom> flow.
65	Elements:
66	  1. <element 1, e.g. "sample preparation icon">
67	  2. <element 2, e.g. "compute pipeline as 3-stage block">
68	  3. <element 3, e.g. "dose-response curve miniature">
69	Text: <one-line headline finding, ≤ 12 words, sans-serif, ≥ 11 pt>.
70	No photorealism, no 3D shading, no decorative elements, no text outside the named labels.
71	```
72	
73	### Route B — Illustrator / Inkscape / TikZ
74	
75	For pipeline / workflow abstracts where every node label needs to match the paper exactly. Vector source committed; PDF rendered at submission time.
76	
77	If TikZ:
78	```latex
79	\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=1.4cm, every node/.style={draw, rounded corners, font=\sffamily}]
80	  \node (x) {Input};
81	  \node[right of=x, node distance=2.5cm] (m) {\methodname};
82	  \node[right of=m, node distance=2.5cm] (r) {Result};
83	  \draw[->, thick] (x) -- (m);
84	  \draw[->, thick] (m) -- (r);
85	  \node[below of=m, node distance=1cm, font=\bfseries] {Headline finding here};
86	\end{tikzpicture}
87	```
88	
89	### Route C — Combine routes
90	
91	Most CNS graphical abstracts mix illustrated mechanism (Route A) + a small hero plot (Route B). Generate them separately, composite in Inkscape or via [[pdf-vector-layout]].
92	
93	## Anti-patterns
94	
95	- Cropping figure-1 of the paper and calling it a graphical abstract. Reviewers can tell.
96	- Putting the abstract text *into* the graphical abstract. The two are separate elements on the journal page.
97	- Stock photos / clip art / royalty-free icons that don't match the paper's specific science. Either commission or generate.
98	- Adding decorative gradients, shadows, 3D bars. CNS aesthetic is restrained.
99	- Forgetting to commit the source (vector / prompt). Camera-ready almost always asks for a re-render.
100	
101	## File outputs
102	
103	Canonical paths under `branches/writer/paper/figures/`:
104	
105	- `graphical_abstract.pdf` — the rendered figure for inclusion.
106	- `graphical_abstract.svg` — vector source if applicable.
107	- `graphical_abstract.prompt.md` — the gpt-image-2.0 prompt if Route A.
108	- `graphical_abstract.tex` — TikZ source if Route B.
109	- `graphical_abstract.png` — 300 dpi preview for slides / draft.
110

# Graphical abstract

Provenance: scientific-writing-kdense ("Every scientific writeup MUST include a graphical abstract"). Mandatory in Cell, Cell Reports, iScience, Cell Press journals; recommended (and increasingly enforced) in Nature, Science, NEJM.

A graphical abstract is a single visual summary of the paper. It appears before/after the text abstract on the journal page and in the table-of-contents listing. Its job is to make a busy reader who scans the TOC stop and read the paper.

## Hard requirements

- **Aspect ratio**: landscape, ~2:1 (Cell prefers 1200×600 px or 5×2.5 inches at 300 dpi). Some Nature sub-journals want square (1:1).
- **Resolution**: 300 dpi at final size for raster; vector PDF preferred when text is present.
- **No more than 5–7 visual elements** in the figure. The reader processes it in < 5 seconds.
- **Self-contained**: must be readable WITHOUT the paper. No "see Fig. 3" cross-references. Define every label inside the abstract figure.
- **Color discipline**: ≤ 4 hues, ColorBrewer or hand-picked. No rainbow.
- **Font**: same family as the body figures; ≥ 9 pt at final size.
- **No text-only**: a graphical abstract that is just a paragraph in a box is not a graphical abstract.

## Content patterns (pick one)

Three patterns cover ≈ 80% of CNS graphical abstracts. Pick the one that matches the paper's contribution.

### Pattern 1 — Workflow / pipeline

Left-to-right or top-to-bottom flow: input → method → output. Use when the paper's contribution is methodological.

```
[Sample] → [Method box (named)] → [Result figure miniature] → [Headline finding text]
```

Implementation: [[figure-spec]] archetype A (boxes-and-arrows), or [[hero-figure-prompt]] for a richer illustrated version.

### Pattern 2 — Mechanism / model

A cartoon of the biological / physical / computational mechanism the paper proposes. Used when the contribution is a *new understanding* rather than a new method.

```
[Before state diagram] → [Mechanism arrow with key molecule / equation] → [After state diagram]
```

Implementation: usually [[hero-figure-prompt]] (gpt-image-2.0 with the persistent prompt file). LaTeX TikZ as fallback.

### Pattern 3 — Headline result

A single "hero plot" — the strongest single panel from the paper, blown up and cleaned. Used when the contribution is a *quantitative finding*.

```
[Hero plot, e.g. dose-response or scaling-law] + [one-line take-home in bold]
```

Implementation: [[academic-plotting]] for the plot + [[hero-figure-prompt]] for the title overlay.

## Generation routes

### Route A — gpt-image-2.0 via hero-figure-prompt

For mechanism / pipeline / illustrated abstracts. Always go through [[hero-figure-prompt]] so the prompt is persisted as `branches/writer/paper/figures/graphical_abstract.prompt.md`. Regeneration must be possible at camera-ready time.

Baseline prompt skeleton (drop into `hero-figure-prompt`):

```
Generate a graphical abstract for a Cell Press submission.
Aspect: landscape 2:1 (1200×600 px).
Style: flat vector illustration, clean lines, scientific aesthetic.
Palette: 3-4 colors, professional pastel + one accent. White background.
Layout: <left/right OR top/bottom> flow.
Elements:
  1. <element 1, e.g. "sample preparation icon">
  2. <element 2, e.g. "compute pipeline as 3-stage block">
  3. <element 3, e.g. "dose-response curve miniature">
Text: <one-line headline finding, ≤ 12 words, sans-serif, ≥ 11 pt>.
No photorealism, no 3D shading, no decorative elements, no text outside the named labels.
```

### Route B — Illustrator / Inkscape / TikZ

For pipeline / workflow abstracts where every node label needs to match the paper exactly. Vector source committed; PDF rendered at submission time.

If TikZ:
```latex
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=1.4cm, every node/.style={draw, rounded corners, font=\sffamily}]
  \node (x) {Input};
  \node[right of=x, node distance=2.5cm] (m) {\methodname};
  \node[right of=m, node distance=2.5cm] (r) {Result};
  \draw[->, thick] (x) -- (m);
  \draw[->, thick] (m) -- (r);
  \node[below of=m, node distance=1cm, font=\bfseries] {Headline finding here};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

### Route C — Combine routes

Most CNS graphical abstracts mix illustrated mechanism (Route A) + a small hero plot (Route B). Generate them separately, composite in Inkscape or via [[pdf-vector-layout]].

## Anti-patterns

- Cropping figure-1 of the paper and calling it a graphical abstract. Reviewers can tell.
- Putting the abstract text *into* the graphical abstract. The two are separate elements on the journal page.
- Stock photos / clip art / royalty-free icons that don't match the paper's specific science. Either commission or generate.
- Adding decorative gradients, shadows, 3D bars. CNS aesthetic is restrained.
- Forgetting to commit the source (vector / prompt). Camera-ready almost always asks for a re-render.

## File outputs

Canonical paths under `branches/writer/paper/figures/`:

- `graphical_abstract.pdf` — the rendered figure for inclusion.
- `graphical_abstract.svg` — vector source if applicable.
- `graphical_abstract.prompt.md` — the gpt-image-2.0 prompt if Route A.
- `graphical_abstract.tex` — TikZ source if Route B.
- `graphical_abstract.png` — 300 dpi preview for slides / draft.

1	# Aesthetic Principles (R-future-2 from user feedback)
2	
3	User-articulated 3-point principle, R-future-2 round 2026-05-17:
4	
5	## Principle 1: Hue coherence
6	
7	**Pick ONE hue family per figure.** All non-grey signals should be hue-
8	coherent: e.g. all blue-cyan-teal, OR all red-pink-magenta, OR all
9	green-mint-yellow. Mixing red AND blue AND green at full saturation in
10	one figure breaks coherence.
11	
12	The exception is **directional contrast**: a grey-dominant figure may use
13	ONE complementary signal hue for the contrast direction. Example: fig3
14	exemplar uses mostly grey + ONE red `#c04040` for treatment direction.
15	
16	Cross-figure exemplars confirming this principle:
17	- diffusion_swiss_roll: pure cyan-teal family (#15c0c0/#15eac0/#15c095)
18	- manifold_holes: mint + cyan family (#c0eac0/#c0eaea)
19	- cellsplicenet_ablation: blue + mint + cyan (all cool family)
20	- fig4-single-cell-systems: warm signal #ea6a40 vs cool signal #6ac0c0
21	  (these ARE directional opposites, intentional diverging cmap)
22	
23	Anti-exemplars (figures that VIOLATE coherence):
24	- Default matplotlib Tab10 cycle (blue/orange/green/red/purple) — every
25	  hue is a distinct hue family. This is what amateur figures default to.
26	- Bar chart with red/blue/green/orange — 4 hue families competing.
27	
28	## Principle 2: Reduced saturation
29	
30	**Saturated colours are visual noise.** Most exemplar figures use
31	~50-70% saturation, not 100%. Specifically:
32	
33	| Element type | Saturation guideline |
34	|---|---|
35	| Background tint | 0-5% saturation (near-white off-white) |
36	| Neutral data (control, baseline) | 0-15% saturation (grey family) |
37	| Signal data (treatment, intervention) | 50-75% saturation, NOT full |
38	| Highlight / emphasis | 75-90%, only when truly directional |
39	
40	The mint family (`#c0eac0`) at ~25% saturation, sitting next to neutral
41	grey, reads CLEAN. The same data plotted in fully saturated `#00ff00`
42	green reads CHEAP.
43	
44	Cross-figure exemplars: diffusion_swiss_roll's #15c0c0 cyan is around
45	60% saturation; manifold_holes' #c0eac0 mint is around 25% saturation;
46	cellsplicenet's #154095 deep blue is around 80% saturation but balanced
47	by surrounding mint and grey neutrals.
48	
49	## Principle 3: Effective display area
50	
51	**Match the visual area of each subplot to the information it carries.**
52	A subplot showing 3 categorical bars does NOT need 180 mm of width.
53	A subplot showing a 30-row heatmap DOES need substantial vertical
54	extent.
55	
56	Two specific failure modes:
57	
58	### 3a: Bar charts wasting axis range
59	The y-axis 0-100 with bars at 60% leaves 40% empty. The user-articulated
60	rule: assuming higher is better, the lowest bar only needs to extend a
61	small amount (just enough to be visible). Implement via
62	`set_ylim(data_min - 0.1*span, data_max + 0.15*span)`. With negative
63	values, NEVER use `(0, ytop)`.
64	
65	### 3b: Subplots over-allocated to data they don't have
66	A bar plot with 3 categories doesn't need a wide canvas. A small bar
67	plot is more honest. Match panel width to data density:
68	
69	```
70	panels with N categorical units: width ~ N * 0.5-0.8 inches
71	panels with longitudinal data (continuous x): width ~ 2-4 inches
72	panels with high-row count (heatmap, raster): height ~ 2-5 inches
73	```
74	
75	Anti-exemplars: R5.C 7-panel candidate's panel D (3 categorical bars in
76	a wide-spanning panel — wasted 60% of horizontal space).
77	
78	## Principle 4 (BACKPACK PROBLEM): Tight composition packing
79	
80	After Principles 1-3 are satisfied per panel, arrange panels in the
81	composite via "backpack packing":
82	
83	- Each panel claims its minimum-needed visual area (Principle 3).
84	- The composite tries to fit all panels into one rectangle with NO empty
85	  cells, NO over-stretched panels, MINIMAL inter-panel gutter (~5% canvas).
86	- Panel hierarchy comes from area allocation: hero panel gets ~30-45%
87	  of canvas; subordinates fill the remainder contiguously.
88	- gridspec subgridspec for nested 2x2 sub-regions tightens spacing when
89	  4 single-cell panels share a corner.
90	
91	This is the layout discipline already encoded in figure-layout-defaults
92	Steps 1-6, with the addition of "minimum-area-per-panel" as the
93	optimisation target.
94	
95	## Principle 5: Novel forms
96	
97	**Beyond bar / line / heatmap / box / violin lies a wider form
98	vocabulary.** When the data has structure that fits a non-standard form,
99	use that form. Examples:
100	
101	- Manifold visualisation (diffusion_swiss_roll, manifold_holes): show
102	  the data's actual geometric structure
103	- Network / matrix combo (atlas-network-matrix): nodes + edges + matrix
104	  in one visual unit
105	- Image plate composite (atlas-image-plates): microscopy + annotation
106	  + scale bar overlay
107	- Radar / polar (atlas-radar-polar, comparison_radar): cross-method
108	  comparison along multiple axes
109	- Forest plot (atlas-forest-interval): per-subset effect + confidence
110	  interval at a glance
111	
112	Form novelty WITHOUT information density is decoration. Form novelty WITH
113	high information density is what makes "human-can't-easily-do-this" figures.
114	
115	## How these principles compose
116	
117	The R-future-2 hierarchy:
118	
119	1. Principle 5 first (pick the right form)
120	2. Principle 3 next (match visual area to information)
121	3. Principle 4 (compose the composite)
122	4. Principles 1+2 (apply hue coherence + reduced saturation)
123	
124	Principles 1+2 alone give "disciplined colour"; Principles 3+4 alone give
125	"clean layout"; Principle 5 alone gives "novel form". All five together
126	approach the user's "beautiful / beyond-human" target.
127	
128	
129	---
130	
131	## R-future-2 user feedback updates (2026-05-17)
132	
133	User graded 6 novel-form exemplars. New sub-rules distilled:
134	
135	### Principle 1 sub-rule: red-vs-teal is a known bad pairing
136	
137	User-flagged on `atlas-network-matrix.png`: heatmap with red and lake-water
138	teal (`#95c0c0`-ish) at the same saturation reads as ugly. Avoid this
139	specific high-complementary pair when both colours are at >40% saturation.
140	
141	Fix when matrix accompanies a network: keep the matrix in the network's
142	hue family (single-hue diverging within teal: `#154040` → `#c0eaea`
143	via TwoSlopeNorm) rather than introducing a complementary signal hue.
144	
145	### Principle 2 sub-rule: stroke weight compensates for low saturation
146	
147	Pastel fills (`#c0eac0`, `#eac0c0`, etc at ~25% saturation) need
148	DOUBLED stroke weight to keep the polygon / shape boundary visible.
149	
150	```python
151	# pastel fill + bumped stroke
152	ax.fill(x, y, color=pastel, alpha=0.25)
153	ax.plot(x, y, color=pastel, linewidth=2.2)  # NOT default 1.0
154	```
155	
156	User-confirmed on `comparison_radar.png`: "the only thing missing is that the
157	outer polygon stroke could be a bit thicker."
158	
159	### Principle 3 sub-rule: legend packing for hybrid composites
160	
161	When a multi-form panel uses a legend, prefer ncol≥2 (multi-column)
162	legend over default single-column. Single-column legend in upper-left
163	of a hybrid panel creates whitespace gap above the data.
164	
165	User-feedback on `cellsplicenet_ablation.png`: "watch the legend setting —
166	splitting it into two columns gets rid of the wasted whitespace in the
167	upper-left corner."
168	
169	```python
170	ax.legend(ncol=2, loc='upper left', frameon=False)
171	```
172	
173	### Principle 4 sub-rule: image plate inter-panel gutter ≤ 2%
174	
175	Standard chart inter-panel gutter is ~5-6% canvas. Image plates
176	(microscopy, spatial transcriptomics, A-Z grid composites) tighten
177	this to ≤2% because the panels are spatially related and the data
178	is the IMAGE itself, not a chart.
179	
180	User-feedback on `atlas-image-plates.png`: "the A-through-P arrangement
181	could be packed tighter."
182	
183	### Principle 5 sub-rule: manifold visualisation > flat chart when geometry IS the message
184\{

12	
13	# Skill — Figure Aesthetic Exemplars
14	
15	## What this skill is
16	
17	A REFERENCE LIBRARY plus a JUDGEMENT WORKFLOW, not a rule list. The
18	companion skills [[figure-layout-defaults]] and [[academic-plotting]]
19	make figures *correct* (no rasterised text, no clashing hues, no empty
20	quadrants). This skill makes figures *beautiful* — closer to what
21	appears in a recent Nature or Cell publication.
22	
23	The premise: rules can constrain (don't do X) but cannot generate
24	(produce Y). Beauty in scientific figures comes from specific,
25	non-rule-encodable choices: a particular palette ratio, a particular
26	typographic rhythm, a particular allocation of white space. The only
27	way to learn these is to STUDY EXEMPLARS — figures that are
28	empirically beautiful — and DIFF YOUR FIGURE AGAINST THEM.
29	
30	## When to invoke
31	
32	- You have a draft figure that satisfies all rule-based skills
33	  (figure-layout-defaults, academic-plotting) but the user / co-author
34	  has flagged it as "looks fine but not beautiful," "lacks any spark,"
35	  or "nowhere near beautiful or good-looking."
36	- You are preparing a Nature / Cell / Science cover-eligible figure
37	  where aesthetic quality matters beyond submittability.
38	- You have iteration budget: this skill expects 2-4 render-revise cycles,
39	  not single-pass rule application.
40	
41	Do NOT invoke if:
42	- The figure is pre-submission and only needs to be SUBMITTABLE (use
43	  rule-based skills only).
44	- You have no iteration budget — exemplar diffing assumes you can
45	  re-render after revision.
46	
47	## Procedure
48	
49	### Step 1 — Render your figure with rule-based skills first
50	
51	Apply [[figure-layout-defaults]] and [[academic-plotting]] FIRST.
52	Render the figure. Save the artefact at, say, `figures/draft1.png`.
53	Don't skip this — exemplar workflow assumes a baseline-correct figure
54	to refine.
55	
56	### Step 2 — Pick the closest exemplar from the gallery
57	
58	Open `gallery/index.md`. Find the exemplar that matches your figure's
59	ARCHETYPE most closely:
60	
61	| Archetype | Exemplar |
62	|---|---|
63	| Multi-panel hero with dose-response + safety + biomarker | `fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich` |
64	| Heatmap-heavy single-cell / dimension reduction | `fig4-single-cell-systems-rich` |
65	| Single-panel grouped bar (ablation, comparison) | `bars_ablation_Cancer` |
66	| Multi-bar cross-category comparison | `correctness_by_category` |
67	| Multi-method sweep with parameter ranges | `results_sweep` |
68	
69	If your figure type isn't in the gallery, pick the *closest* archetype
70	and note the gap. Future R rounds extend the gallery.
71	
72	### Step 3 — Open the exemplar's annotation card
73	
74	Each exemplar in `gallery/` has a sibling `<exemplar>.annotation.md`
75	file documenting:
76	
77	- **Extracted palette**: actual hex values dominant in the rendered
78	  figure (not generic rules, but what THIS exemplar uses)
79	- **Palette ratio**: percentage of pixels in each colour family
80	  (background grey vs signal accent vs subordinate hue)
81	- **Typography**: font sizes per element class (axis label, panel
82	  letter, caption note, in-panel annotation)
83	- **White space allocation**: ratio of panel area to whitespace
84	- **Visual rhythm**: where the eye lands first, second, third
85	- **What makes this figure work**: 3-5 bullet points naming the
86	  specific design choices that differentiate it from a "correct but
87	  bland" figure
88	
89	### Step 4 — Diff your figure against the exemplar
90	
91	Open both side-by-side (Playwright HTML page or local image viewer).
92	Identify the 3 biggest visual deltas. Examples:
93	
94	- Your palette has 4 saturated hues; exemplar has 1 signal + 2 neutrals
95	  + heavy background grey.
96	- Your panel labels are 7-pt bold; exemplar uses 9-pt bold with extra
97	  weight contrast.
98	- Your hero panel fills 30% of canvas; exemplar's hero fills 45%.
99	- Your bars use 0-100 axis with 35% empty top; exemplar zooms to data
100	  range with 10% headroom.
101	
102	Rank the 3 deltas by aesthetic impact (typography is usually high
103	impact; precise hex value is usually low).
104	
105	### Step 5 — Revise the script to address top-3 deltas
106	
107	DON'T re-do the figure from scratch. Edit the existing script to
108	address only the 3 named deltas. Re-render to `draft2.png`.
109	
110	### Step 6 — Re-diff and decide
111	
112	Compare draft2 vs the exemplar again. If the gap has closed
113	substantially, stop. If 1-2 of the 3 deltas remain, do one more
114	iteration. **Cap at 4 iterations** — beyond that, you're polishing
115	what should have been redesigned.
116	
117	## What this skill does NOT do
118	
119	- It does NOT rewrite [[figure-layout-defaults]] or [[academic-plotting]].
120	  Use those first; this skill is the polish step.
121	- It does NOT generate exemplars. The gallery is curated from real
122	  published figures.
123	- It does NOT replace human aesthetic judgment for a venue cover. For
124	  Nature cover-grade figures, a human designer still adds ~20% beyond
125	  what this workflow produces.
126	- It does NOT tell you a specific colour to use. It tells you ratios
127	  and exemplar precedents; the runner picks specific hex values
128	  matching the manuscript palette family.
129	
130	## Pitfalls
131	
132	- **Picking the wrong exemplar.** If your figure is a single-panel
133	  bar chart and you diff against `fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich` (a
134	  4-panel hero composite), the deltas will be misleading. Match
135	  archetype first.
136	- **Copying the exemplar's palette pixel-perfect.** The point is to
137	  learn the RATIO and the FAMILY, not to literally copy hex values
138	  from a different paper's figure. Translate the lesson to your
139	  manuscript's palette.
140	- **Endless iteration.** 4-iteration cap is a hard limit. If you can't
141	  close the gap in 4 cycles, the figure needs structural redesign,
142	  not aesthetic polish.
143	- **Ignoring the rule-based baseline.** This skill ASSUMES rules are
144	  already applied. If you skip [[figure-layout-defaults]] first, the
145	  exemplar diff becomes "your figure is wrong AND ugly" — fix wrong
146	  first.
147	
148	## Output habit
149	
150	When delivering a polished figure, document:
151	
152	```
153	# aesthetic exemplar workflow
154	exemplar_used: gallery/fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich.png
155	top-3 deltas addressed:
156	  1. palette: removed 2 redundant hues, kept 1 signal + 2 neutrals
157	  2. typography: panel letters 8pt -> 9pt bold, axis labels 6.5 -> 7pt
158	  3. hero panel area: 30% -> 42% via gridspec width_ratios
159	iterations: 2
160	remaining gaps (acknowledged): exemplar uses subtle background tint
161	  in hero panel; we did not replicate (manuscript palette has no
162	  near-white tint family available)
163	```
164	
165	## Provenance
166	
167	Distilled from SkillTest R6.A user feedback. The R1-R6 figure rounds
168	established that rule-based skills (figure-layout-defaults,
169	academic-plotting) plateau at "correct / submittable" but do not reach
170	"beautiful." The aesthetic-exemplar paradigm — REFERENCE LIBRARY +
171	DIFF WORKFLOW — is the SkillTest hypothesis for closing that gap.
172	
173	R-future validation: re-run R5.C 7-panel or R6.A 9-panel through this
174	workflow + user visual review. If the user grades the result
175	materially better than rule-only candidate, this paradigm is
176	load-bearing. If not, the gap requires a vision-capable iterative
177	loop or a human designer.

# Skill — Figure Aesthetic Exemplars

## What this skill is

A REFERENCE LIBRARY plus a JUDGEMENT WORKFLOW, not a rule list. The
companion skills [[figure-layout-defaults]] and [[academic-plotting]]
make figures *correct* (no rasterised text, no clashing hues, no empty
quadrants). This skill makes figures *beautiful* — closer to what
appears in a recent Nature or Cell publication.

(produce Y). Beauty in scientific figures comes from specific,
non-rule-encodable choices: a particular palette ratio, a particular
typographic rhythm, a particular allocation of white space. The only
way to learn these is to STUDY EXEMPLARS — figures that are
empirically beautiful — and DIFF YOUR FIGURE AGAINST THEM.

## When to invoke

- You have a draft figure that satisfies all rule-based skills
  (figure-layout-defaults, academic-plotting) but the user / co-author
  has flagged it as "looks fine but not beautiful," "lacks any spark,"
  or "nowhere near beautiful or good-looking."
- You are preparing a Nature / Cell / Science cover-eligible figure
  where aesthetic quality matters beyond submittability.
- You have iteration budget: this skill expects 2-4 render-revise cycles,
  not single-pass rule application.

Do NOT invoke if:
- The figure is pre-submission and only needs to be SUBMITTABLE (use
  rule-based skills only).
- You have no iteration budget — exemplar diffing assumes you can
  re-render after revision.

## Procedure

### Step 1 — Render your figure with rule-based skills first

Apply [[figure-layout-defaults]] and [[academic-plotting]] FIRST.
Render the figure. Save the artefact at, say, `figures/draft1.png`.
Don't skip this — exemplar workflow assumes a baseline-correct figure
to refine.

### Step 2 — Pick the closest exemplar from the gallery

| Archetype | Exemplar |
|---|---|
| Multi-panel hero with dose-response + safety + biomarker | `fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich` |
| Heatmap-heavy single-cell / dimension reduction | `fig4-single-cell-systems-rich` |
| Single-panel grouped bar (ablation, comparison) | `bars_ablation_Cancer` |
| Multi-bar cross-category comparison | `correctness_by_category` |
| Multi-method sweep with parameter ranges | `results_sweep` |

If your figure type isn't in the gallery, pick the *closest* archetype
and note the gap. Future R rounds extend the gallery.

### Step 3 — Open the exemplar's annotation card

Each exemplar in `gallery/` has a sibling `<exemplar>.annotation.md`
file documenting:

- **Extracted palette**: actual hex values dominant in the rendered
  figure (not generic rules, but what THIS exemplar uses)
- **Palette ratio**: percentage of pixels in each colour family
  (background grey vs signal accent vs subordinate hue)
- **Typography**: font sizes per element class (axis label, panel
  letter, caption note, in-panel annotation)
- **White space allocation**: ratio of panel area to whitespace
- **Visual rhythm**: where the eye lands first, second, third
- **What makes this figure work**: 3-5 bullet points naming the
  specific design choices that differentiate it from a "correct but
  bland" figure

### Step 4 — Diff your figure against the exemplar

Open both side-by-side (Playwright HTML page or local image viewer).
Identify the 3 biggest visual deltas. Examples:

- Your palette has 4 saturated hues; exemplar has 1 signal + 2 neutrals
  + heavy background grey.
- Your panel labels are 7-pt bold; exemplar uses 9-pt bold with extra
  weight contrast.
- Your hero panel fills 30% of canvas; exemplar's hero fills 45%.
- Your bars use 0-100 axis with 35% empty top; exemplar zooms to data
  range with 10% headroom.

Rank the 3 deltas by aesthetic impact (typography is usually high
impact; precise hex value is usually low).

### Step 5 — Revise the script to address top-3 deltas

DON'T re-do the figure from scratch. Edit the existing script to
address only the 3 named deltas. Re-render to `draft2.png`.

### Step 6 — Re-diff and decide

Compare draft2 vs the exemplar again. If the gap has closed
substantially, stop. If 1-2 of the 3 deltas remain, do one more

## What this skill does NOT do

- It does NOT rewrite [[figure-layout-defaults]] or [[academic-plotting]].

- It does NOT generate exemplars. The gallery is curated from real
  published figures.
- It does NOT replace human aesthetic judgment for a venue cover. For

  what this workflow produces.
- It does NOT tell you a specific colour to use. It tells you ratios
  and exemplar precedents; the runner picks specific hex values
  matching the manuscript palette family.

- **Picking the wrong exemplar.** If your figure is a single-panel

  4-panel hero composite), the deltas will be misleading. Match
  archetype first.
- **Copying the exemplar's palette pixel-perfect.** The point is to

  manuscript's palette.
- **Endless iteration.** 4-iteration cap is a hard limit. If you can't
  close the gap in 4 cycles, the figure needs structural redesign,
  not aesthetic polish.
- **Ignoring the rule-based baseline.** This skill ASSUMES rules are
  already applied. If you skip [[figure-layout-defaults]] first, the
  exemplar diff becomes "your figure is wrong AND ugly" — fix wrong
  first.

## Output habit

When delivering a polished figure, document:

```
# aesthetic exemplar workflow
exemplar_used: gallery/fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich.png
top-3 deltas addressed:
  1. palette: removed 2 redundant hues, kept 1 signal + 2 neutrals
  2. typography: panel letters 8pt -> 9pt bold, axis labels 6.5 -> 7pt
  3. hero panel area: 30% -> 42% via gridspec width_ratios
iterations: 2
remaining gaps (acknowledged): exemplar uses subtle background tint
  in hero panel; we did not replicate (manuscript palette has no
  near-white tint family available)
```

## Provenance

Distilled from SkillTest R6.A user feedback. The R1-R6 figure rounds
established that rule-based skills (figure-layout-defaults,
academic-plotting) plateau at "correct / submittable" but do not reach
"beautiful." The aesthetic-exemplar paradigm — REFERENCE LIBRARY +
DIFF WORKFLOW — is the SkillTest hypothesis for closing that gap.

R-future validation: re-run R5.C 7-panel or R6.A 9-panel through this
workflow + user visual review. If the user grades the result
materially better than rule-only candidate, this paradigm is
load-bearing. If not, the gap requires a vision-capable iterative
loop or a human designer.

# Graphical abstract

Provenance: scientific-writing-kdense ("Every scientific writeup MUST include a graphical abstract"). Mandatory in Cell, Cell Reports, iScience, Cell Press journals; recommended (and increasingly enforced) in Nature, Science, NEJM.

A graphical abstract is a single visual summary of the paper. It appears before/after the text abstract on the journal page and in the table-of-contents listing. Its job is to make a busy reader who scans the TOC stop and read the paper.

## Hard requirements

- **Aspect ratio**: landscape, ~2:1 (Cell prefers 1200×600 px or 5×2.5 inches at 300 dpi). Some Nature sub-journals want square (1:1).
- **Resolution**: 300 dpi at final size for raster; vector PDF preferred when text is present.
- **No more than 5–7 visual elements** in the figure. The reader processes it in < 5 seconds.
- **Self-contained**: must be readable WITHOUT the paper. No "see Fig. 3" cross-references. Define every label inside the abstract figure.
- **Color discipline**: ≤ 4 hues, ColorBrewer or hand-picked. No rainbow.
- **Font**: same family as the body figures; ≥ 9 pt at final size.
- **No text-only**: a graphical abstract that is just a paragraph in a box is not a graphical abstract.

## Content patterns (pick one)

Three patterns cover ≈ 80% of CNS graphical abstracts. Pick the one that matches the paper's contribution.

### Pattern 1 — Workflow / pipeline

Left-to-right or top-to-bottom flow: input → method → output. Use when the paper's contribution is methodological.

```
[Sample] → [Method box (named)] → [Result figure miniature] → [Headline finding text]
```

Implementation: [[figure-spec]] archetype A (boxes-and-arrows), or [[hero-figure-prompt]] for a richer illustrated version.

### Pattern 2 — Mechanism / model

A cartoon of the biological / physical / computational mechanism the paper proposes. Used when the contribution is a *new understanding* rather than a new method.

```
[Before state diagram] → [Mechanism arrow with key molecule / equation] → [After state diagram]
```

Implementation: usually [[hero-figure-prompt]] (gpt-image-2.0 with the persistent prompt file). LaTeX TikZ as fallback.

### Pattern 3 — Headline result

A single "hero plot" — the strongest single panel from the paper, blown up and cleaned. Used when the contribution is a *quantitative finding*.

```
[Hero plot, e.g. dose-response or scaling-law] + [one-line take-home in bold]
```

Implementation: [[academic-plotting]] for the plot + [[hero-figure-prompt]] for the title overlay.

## Generation routes

### Route A — gpt-image-2.0 via hero-figure-prompt

For mechanism / pipeline / illustrated abstracts. Always go through [[hero-figure-prompt]] so the prompt is persisted as `branches/writer/paper/figures/graphical_abstract.prompt.md`. Regeneration must be possible at camera-ready time.

Baseline prompt skeleton (drop into `hero-figure-prompt`):

```
Generate a graphical abstract for a Cell Press submission.
Aspect: landscape 2:1 (1200×600 px).
Style: flat vector illustration, clean lines, scientific aesthetic.
Palette: 3-4 colors, professional pastel + one accent. White background.
Layout: <left/right OR top/bottom> flow.
Elements:
  1. <element 1, e.g. "sample preparation icon">
  2. <element 2, e.g. "compute pipeline as 3-stage block">
  3. <element 3, e.g. "dose-response curve miniature">
Text: <one-line headline finding, ≤ 12 words, sans-serif, ≥ 11 pt>.
No photorealism, no 3D shading, no decorative elements, no text outside the named labels.
```

### Route B — Illustrator / Inkscape / TikZ

For pipeline / workflow abstracts where every node label needs to match the paper exactly. Vector source committed; PDF rendered at submission time.

If TikZ:
```latex
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=1.4cm, every node/.style={draw, rounded corners, font=\sffamily}]
  \node (x) {Input};
  \node[right of=x, node distance=2.5cm] (m) {\methodname};
  \node[right of=m, node distance=2.5cm] (r) {Result};
  \draw[->, thick] (x) -- (m);
  \draw[->, thick] (m) -- (r);
  \node[below of=m, node distance=1cm, font=\bfseries] {Headline finding here};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

### Route C — Combine routes

Most CNS graphical abstracts mix illustrated mechanism (Route A) + a small hero plot (Route B). Generate them separately, composite in Inkscape or via [[pdf-vector-layout]].

## Anti-patterns

- Cropping figure-1 of the paper and calling it a graphical abstract. Reviewers can tell.
- Putting the abstract text *into* the graphical abstract. The two are separate elements on the journal page.
- Stock photos / clip art / royalty-free icons that don't match the paper's specific science. Either commission or generate.
- Adding decorative gradients, shadows, 3D bars. CNS aesthetic is restrained.
- Forgetting to commit the source (vector / prompt). Camera-ready almost always asks for a re-render.

## File outputs

Canonical paths under `branches/writer/paper/figures/`:

- `graphical_abstract.pdf` — the rendered figure for inclusion.
- `graphical_abstract.svg` — vector source if applicable.
- `graphical_abstract.prompt.md` — the gpt-image-2.0 prompt if Route A.
- `graphical_abstract.tex` — TikZ source if Route B.
- `graphical_abstract.png` — 300 dpi preview for slides / draft.

content.content:
1	---
2	slug: figure-layout-defaults
3	summary: Default 4-panel hero is width_ratios=[2,1,1] + bottom-spanning D; figsize in inches first, mm only at submission; no empty quadrants, no over-stretched panels, no constrained_layout collapse.
4	layer: logical
5	tools:
6	version: 2
7	status: active
8	supersedes:
9	references: academic-plotting, pdf-vector-layout
10	provenance: SkillTest-R1.A-and-R1.B-case-multi-panel
11	---
12	
13	# Skill — Figure Layout Defaults
14	
15	Layout discipline that two existing CNS figure skills (nature-figure,
16	scientific-figure-making) both fail to teach. The content-discipline rules
17	they DO teach (rcParams, palette, editable text) are covered separately in
18	[[academic-plotting]]; this skill covers the geometry decisions both skills
19	get wrong on multi-panel composites.
20	
21	## When to invoke
22	
23	- Any multi-panel figure (≥2 panels) for a paper, not a quick exploratory plot.
24	- Any single-panel figure where the brief specifies submission dimensions
25	  (single-column ~85 mm, two-column ~180 mm) — the figsize-vs-density rule applies.
26	- Whenever a figure script includes legend + caption + statistical brackets
27	  in the same axes.
28	
29	## Procedure
30	
31	### Step 1 — Pick the right grid before the figsize
32	
33	For 2-panel: side-by-side (`subplots(1, 2)`) is almost always right.
34	
35	For 3-panel: row of 3 (`subplots(1, 3)`) or 2-on-top + 1-below is almost
36	always right.
37	
38	For 4-panel: **default to `gridspec(2, 3, width_ratios=[2,1,1],
39	height_ratios=[1.4, 1])` with `ax_d = gs[1, :]`.** A wide A on the upper-left,
40	B and C beside it on the upper-right, D spanning the bottom row.
41	
42	```python
43	fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 6))   # inches; tune later
44	gs = fig.add_gridspec(2, 3, width_ratios=[2, 1, 1], height_ratios=[1.4, 1])
45	ax_a = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 0])     # hero
46	ax_b = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 1])     # subordinate
47	ax_c = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 2])     # subordinate
48	ax_d = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, :])     # bottom-spanning
49	```
50	
51	This is the layout SkillTest R1.A and R1.B baselines used by accident, and
52	the user confirmed both as "very polished and refined".
53	
54	For 5-panel: **default to `gridspec(3, 3)` with hero=`gs[0:2, 0:2]`**
55	(R4.B confirmed: user called this layout "remarkably good-looking and accurate"):
56	
57	```python
58	fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 7.5))  # inches; tune later
59	gs = fig.add_gridspec(3, 3)
60	ax_a = fig.add_subplot(gs[0:2, 0:2])  # hero — 4 cells, dominant
61	ax_b = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 2])      # subordinate top-right
62	ax_c = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 2])      # subordinate mid-right
63	ax_d = fig.add_subplot(gs[2, 0])      # bottom-left single
64	ax_e = fig.add_subplot(gs[2, 1:])     # bottom-spanning 2 cells
65	```
66	
67	For 6-panel and beyond, the rule generalises: designate a 4-cell hero
68	region, arrange subordinates around it as a contiguous remainder, no
69	empty cells, no over-stretching.
70	
71	Custom asymmetric grids (`gs[0:2, 0:2] + gs[1:, 2:]`, etc) are NOT the
72	default. Only design a custom grid if:
73	1. The default has been tried and demonstrably fails for the specific brief, OR
74	2. The brief explicitly requests a non-standard layout.
75	
76	### Step 2 — Pick figsize in inches first
77	
78	Default sizes:
79	- single panel: `figsize=(6, 4)` inches
80	- 2-panel side-by-side: `figsize=(10, 4)`
81	- 4-panel hero (above): `figsize=(11, 6)`
82	
83	Compress to exact mm only at submission packaging stage, AFTER the layout
84	has been visually confirmed. Do NOT lead with `figsize=(85/25.4, 58/25.4)`
85	when the figure has legend, caption, and significance brackets to fit —
86	that produced unreadable cramming in SkillTest R1.A.
87	
88	### Step 3 — Layout-budget triage when figsize is tight
89	
90	When figsize is journal-strict (single column ~85 mm wide, ~60-80 mm tall)
91	you cannot fit all of:
92	- above-axes legend
93	- below-axes caption
94	- in-axes significance brackets
95	- 4-series hatched bars at 7-pt body
96	
97	Pick at most one of (legend / caption / sig brackets) inside the figure;
98	move the others external (caption goes to figure metadata or paper text;
99	significance summary goes to caption). The default for tight figsize is
100	"legend in-axes, caption external."
101	
102	### Step 4 — Sanity-check before declaring done
103	
104	After saving:
105	- Open the rendered PNG. Don't trust the script — open the file.
106	- Verify legend doesn't overlap data.
107	- Verify all axis labels are visible (no truncation at the figure edge).
108	- Verify no font fallback you didn't intend (`fc-list :family | grep Arial`
109	  on Linux CI; on macOS Arial is usually resident).
110	- Verify hero panel is genuinely dominant by area, not just width.
111	- Verify no quadrant is empty whitespace.
112	- Verify no panel is over-stretched (3 categorical bars in a 180 mm-wide
113	  panel is the failure case).
114	
115	### Step 5 — Pack nested 2x2 sub-regions tightly
116	
117	When 4 single-cell subordinate panels would share a 2x2 sub-region of a
118	larger grid (e.g. B/C/D/E at gs[0,2], gs[0,3], gs[1,2], gs[1,3] within
119	a 4x4 grid), the matplotlib default wspace/hspace is proportional to the
120	FULL grid (4 columns) and produces visible whitespace between the 4
121	related panels. The visual effect is "scattered" rather than packed.
122	
123	**Fix:** wrap the 2x2 sub-region in its own subgridspec:
124	
125	```python
126	sub_BCDE = gs[0:2, 2:4].subgridspec(2, 2, wspace=0.4, hspace=0.4)
127	ax_b = fig.add_subplot(sub_BCDE[0, 0])
128	ax_c = fig.add_subplot(sub_BCDE[0, 1])
129	ax_d = fig.add_subplot(sub_BCDE[1, 0])
130	ax_e = fig.add_subplot(sub_BCDE[1, 1])
131	```
132	
133	This packs B/C/D/E with their own (tighter) spacing while preserving the
134	larger grid's wspace/hspace for the hero+subordinate boundary.
135	
136	User-confirmed at R6.A: without this rule, "the upper-right region had loose, drifting chart elements with too much whitespace in the middle".
137	
138	### Step 6 — Tune y-axis range to data density, not nominal range
139	
140	When a bar / dot / violin / box plot's data spans less than ~70% of the
141	default axis range, the rendered figure has visible empty space above
142	or below the data. The default axis range is for the wrong message:
143	it shows "scale" but the panel's job is to show "difference."
144	
145	**Default rule:** if `(data_max - data_min) / nominal_range < 0.7`
146	**OR data spans both negative and positive values**, re-scale axis to
147	`[data_min - 0.1*span, data_max + 0.15*span]`.
148	
149	```python
150	data_min, data_max = bar_values.min(), bar_values.max()
151	span = data_max - data_min
152	ax.set_ylim(data_min - 0.1 * span, data_max + 0.15 * span)
153	```
154	
155	**Important: never default to `set_ylim(0, ytop)` when data includes
156	negative values.** That clips the negative bars to zero height — they
157	disappear visually. R-future validation R5.C 7-panel and R-future
158	aesthetic-polished both hit this bug: Vehicle data was -3% (negative
159	tumour reduction), CompoundX was 65%, both candidates used `(0, ytop)`
160	floor and the Vehicle bars vanished. User explicitly flagged: "the gray bars
161	became completely invisible — the y-axis range was wrong".
162	
163	The 0-floor applies ONLY when data is naturally non-negative AND the
164	absolute level is part of the message. Negative-value bars require
165	data-range scaling.
166	
167	The 15% top headroom leaves room for significance-marker brackets above
168	the highest bar.
169	
170	**Counter-rule:** if the y-axis is a probability or fraction (0 to 1)
171	and the data is naturally near the boundaries, AND the absolute level
172	is part of the scientific claim (e.g. "achieved 95% accuracy"), keep
173	the full 0-1 range. The rule applies when the visual emphasis IS the
174	difference between groups, not the absolute level.
175	
176	User-confirmed at R6.A: bar chart with values ~65 and ~-3 on y-axis 0-100
177	leaves ~35% empty top — "those bars are drawn so long they convey nothing".
178	The right visual is "the shortest bar should be barely visible; even a small
179	extra height makes the difference legible".
180	
181	If any check fails:
182	- `constrained_layout collapsed to zero` warning fired? **Hard fail.**
183	  Re-render at larger figsize. Do not save it; do not assume `bbox_inches="tight"`
184	  recovers (in SkillTest R1.A it didn't).
185	- Legend overlapping bars? Move it above or below the axes, or make the
186	  figure taller.
187	- Empty quadrant? Either extend an existing panel into the empty cell or
188	  pick a smaller grid.
189	
190	## Pitfalls
191	
192	- Designing a custom asymmetric `gridspec` because it "looks more
193	  sophisticated." SkillTest R1.A nature-figure produced a grid with empty
194	  `gs[2, 0:2]`; R1.B scientific-figure-making produced cramped B/C and
195	  over-stretched D. Both lost to baseline's standard pattern.
196	- Treating `constrained_layout collapsed to zero` as a cosmetic warning.
197	  It means matplotlib could not fit the requested elements at the requested
198	  figsize and gave up. `bbox_inches="tight"` may save a file but the
199	  rendered figure will be cramped beyond legibility.
200	- Setting `figsize=(85/25.4, 58/25.4)` because the brief says "single
201	  column ~85 mm" without checking whether 58 mm of height has room for
202	  legend + caption + density. The brief specifies *width*; height needs
203	  to be derived from content density.
204	- Allocating equal panel area to panels with very different data density.
205	  3 categorical bars do NOT need a 180 mm-wide panel. Match panel size to
206	  what the panel has to show.
207	- Letting the legend shrink to 5-pt to fit the squeezed B/C corner.
208	  Sub-spec text undermines the editable-text rcParams gain.
209	
210	## Output habit
211	
212	When delivering a figure, include in the script comments or accompanying
213	`figure_spec.json`:
214	
215	```
216	# layout: gridspec(2, 3, width_ratios=[2,1,1], height_ratios=[1.4, 1])
217	# figsize: 11x6 inches (= 280 x 152 mm; tune to 180 x 100 mm at submission packaging)
218	# layout-budget choice: legend above-axes, caption external in paper text
219	# sanity checks passed: no overlap, no truncation, hero dominant by area
220	```
221	
222	This makes the layout reasoning legible to the next editor or reviewer.
223	
224	## Provenance
225	
226	Distilled from SkillTest R1 visual-review evidence:
227	- R1.A nature-figure: case-bar (mm-figsize cramming, `constrained_layout
228	  collapsed to zero`), case-multi-panel (empty `gs[2, 0:2]` quadrant).
229	- R1.B scientific-figure-making: case-bar (lighter rule set avoided

# Skill — Figure Layout Defaults

Layout discipline that two existing CNS figure skills (nature-figure,
scientific-figure-making) both fail to teach. The content-discipline rules
they DO teach (rcParams, palette, editable text) are covered separately in
[[academic-plotting]]; this skill covers the geometry decisions both skills
get wrong on multi-panel composites.

## When to invoke

- Any multi-panel figure (≥2 panels) for a paper, not a quick exploratory plot.
- Any single-panel figure where the brief specifies submission dimensions
  (single-column ~85 mm, two-column ~180 mm) — the figsize-vs-density rule applies.
- Whenever a figure script includes legend + caption + statistical brackets
  in the same axes.

## Procedure

### Step 1 — Pick the right grid before the figsize

For 2-panel: side-by-side (`subplots(1, 2)`) is almost always right.

For 3-panel: row of 3 (`subplots(1, 3)`) or 2-on-top + 1-below is almost
always right.

For 4-panel: **default to `gridspec(2, 3, width_ratios=[2,1,1],
height_ratios=[1.4, 1])` with `ax_d = gs[1, :]`.** A wide A on the upper-left,
B and C beside it on the upper-right, D spanning the bottom row.

I have all the context needed. Now I'll design the graphical abstract. Based on the brief:
- **Pattern 1: Workflow/Pipeline** — RetroDiff is a methodological contribution
- Layout: left-to-right flow: Protein Pocket → ChEMBL Retrieval (ECFP4) → Diffusion Decoder → Generated Molecules + 3 metric callouts

{"file_path": "/Users/mjm/MinionsOS/outline/ExperimentsOfMinionsos/FigureDraw3/sessions/minionsos-v3/cns-graphical-abstract/20260524T184716Z/workspace/gen_figure.py", "content": """"
RetroDiff graphical abstract — Cell-tier submission.
Pattern: Workflow (Pipeline) — Pattern 1 from CNS graphical-abstract guidance.
Layout: landscape 2:1 (~12x6 inches) left-to-right flow.

Elements:
  1. Protein binding pocket icon (input)
  2. ChEMBL retrieval block (ECFP4 top-k)
  3. Diffusion decoder block (method core)
  4. Generated molecule output
  5. Three metric callouts + headline sentence

Color family: cool teal/mint/slate — all ~25-70% saturation.
Font stack: Arial → Helvetica → DejaVu Sans (set once in rcParams).
"""

import matplotlib as mpl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import matplotlib.patheffects as pe
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.patches import FancyBboxPatch, FancyArrowPatch
from matplotlib.path import Path
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

# ── Font stack locked once ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
mpl.rcParams.update({
    "font.family": "sans-serif",
    "font.sans-serif": ["Arial", "Helvetica", "DejaVu Sans", "Liberation Sans"],
    "pdf.fonttype": 42,
    "svg.fonttype": "none",
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.spines.left": False,
    "axes.spines.bottom": False,
})

# ── Palette (cool teal/mint/slate family, ≤4 hues) ─────────────────────────
C_TEAL      = "#2a9d8f"   # ~70% sat, main accent
C_MINT      = "#a8dacc"   # ~35% sat, retrieval fill
C_SLATE     = "#457b9d"   # ~65% sat, diffusion block
C_LAVENDER  = "#c0d6e8"   # ~25% sat, pocket fill
C_GREY      = "#6c757d"   # neutral text / arrows
C_DARKGREY  = "#2d3436"   # headline text
C_WHITE     = "#ffffff"
C_OFFWHITE  = "#f8f9fa"   # figure background

# Metric accent colors (still cool-family)
C_METRIC1   = "#1b7a6e"   # dark teal for +18%
C_METRIC2   = "#2171a3"   # dark slate for 60%
C_METRIC3   = "#264653"   # dark navy for R²>0.9

# ── Figure setup ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6), facecolor=C_WHITE)
fig.patch.set_facecolor(C_WHITE)

# Single axes covering full canvas — we draw everything manually
ax = fig.add_axes([0, 0, 1, 1])
ax.set_xlim(0, 12)
ax.set_ylim(0, 6)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_facecolor(C_WHITE)
ax.axis("off")

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Helper functions
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def rounded_box(ax, x, y, w, h, fc, ec, lw=1.5, pad=0.15, label=None,
                sublabel=None, fontsize=10, subsize=8):
    """Draw a rounded rectangle with centered text."""
    box = FancyBboxPatch(
        (x - w/2, y - h/2), w, h,
        boxstyle=f"round,pad={pad}",
        facecolor=fc, edgecolor=ec, linewidth=lw, zorder=3
    )
    ax.add_patch(box)
    if label:
        ax.text(x, y + (0.18 if sublabel else 0), label,
                ha="center", va="center", fontsize=fontsize,
                fontweight="bold", color=C_DARKGREY, zorder=4)
    if sublabel:
        ax.text(x, y - 0.28, sublabel,
                ha="center", va="center", fontsize=subsize,
                color=C_GREY, zorder=4, style="italic")


def flow_arrow(ax, x1, x2, y, color=C_GREY, lw=2.0):
    """Draw a horizontal flow arrow."""
    ax.annotate("",
        xy=(x2, y), xytext=(x1, y),
        arrowprops=dict(
            arrowstyle="-|>",
            color=color,
            lw=lw,
            mutation_scale=16,
        ),
        zorder=5
    )


def metric_badge(ax, x, y, value_text, desc_text, color):
    """Compact metric callout."""
    # Value in large bold
    ax.text(x, y + 0.22, value_text,
            ha="center", va="center", fontsize=14,
            fontweight="bold", color=color, zorder=6)
    # Description below
    ax.text(x, y - 0.22, desc_text,
            ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5,
            color=C_GREY, zorder=6)
    # Underline rule
    ax.plot([x - 0.55, x + 0.55], [y - 0.05, y - 0.05],
            color=color, lw=1.0, alpha=0.5, zorder=5)


def pocket_icon(ax, cx, cy, r=0.65):
    """Schematic protein binding pocket as arc + small circles."""
    # Outer arc (pocket mouth)
    theta = np.linspace(np.pi * 0.15, np.pi * 0.85, 80)
    xs = cx + r * np.cos(theta)
    ys = cy + r * np.sin(theta) + 0.1
    ax.fill_between(xs, ys, cy - 0.3,
                    color=C_LAVENDER, alpha=0.55, zorder=2)
    ax.plot(xs, ys, color=C_SLATE, lw=2.0, zorder=3)
    # "residue" dots around pocket
    for angle, rad in [(0.25, 0.58), (0.5, 0.58), (0.75, 0.58)]:
        px = cx + rad * np.cos(np.pi * angle + np.pi * 0.15)
        py = cy + rad * np.sin(np.pi * angle + np.pi * 0.15) + 0.1
        ax.plot(px, py, "o", ms=6, color=C_SLATE, zorder=4)
    # ligand placeholder (small hexagon)
    n = 6
    hx = [cx + 0.18 * np.cos(2*np.pi*k/n) for k in range(n+1)]
    hy = [cy + 0.18 * np.sin(2*np.pi*k/n) + 0.02 for k in range(n+1)]
    ax.fill(hx, hy, color=C_TEAL, alpha=0.7, zorder=4)
    ax.plot(hx, hy, color=C_TEAL, lw=1.2, zorder=5)


def molecule_icon(ax, cx, cy, color=C_TEAL, scale=0.55):
    """Schematic 2D molecule (benzene-like ring + substituents)."""
    n = 6
    r = 0.28 * scale / 0.55
    # ring
    ring_x = [cx + r * np.cos(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6) for k in range(n+1)]
    ring_y = [cy + r * np.sin(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6) for k in range(n+1)]
    ax.fill(ring_x, ring_y, color=color, alpha=0.25, zorder=3)
    ax.plot(ring_x, ring_y, color=color, lw=1.8, zorder=4)
    # substituent sticks
    for k in [0, 2, 4]:
        bx = cx + r * np.cos(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6)
        by = cy + r * np.sin(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6)
        ex = bx + 0.22 * np.cos(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6)
        ey = by + 0.22 * np.sin(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6)
        ax.plot([bx, ex], [by, ey], color=color, lw=1.5, zorder=4)
        ax.plot(ex, ey, "o", ms=4, color=color, alpha=0.8, zorder=5)


def chembl_icon(ax, cx, cy):
    """Mini database icon: stacked cylinder-like rects."""
    w, h = 0.7, 0.18
    gap = 0.05
    colors = [C_MINT, "#8ecfc0", "#6ab8a8"]
    for i, c in enumerate(colors):
        yy = cy + (1 - i) * (h + gap)
        box = FancyBboxPatch(
            (cx - w/2, yy - h/2), w, h,
            boxstyle="round,pad=0.04",
            facecolor=c, edgecolor=C_TEAL, linewidth=1.0, zorder=3
        )
        ax.add_patch(box)
    # top ellipse cap
    ellipse = mpatches.Ellipse((cx, cy + (h + gap) + h/2), w, 0.15,
                                facecolor="#6ab8a8", edgecolor=C_TEAL,
                                linewidth=1.0, zorder=4)
    ax.add_patch(ellipse)


def diffusion_icon(ax, cx, cy):
    """Schematic diffusion denoising: noisy → clean molecule trajectory."""
    # trajectory arc
    t = np.linspace(0, 1, 60)
    noise_amp = 0.22 * (1 - t)**1.4
    x_traj = cx - 0.8 + 1.6 * t
    y_traj = cy + noise_amp * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t * 3)
    ax.plot(x_traj, y_traj, color=C_SLATE, lw=2.0, alpha=0.7, zorder=3,
            solid_capstyle="round")
    # noise cloud on left
    rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
    noise_x = cx - 0.72 + rng.normal(0, 0.12, 12)
    noise_y = cy + rng.normal(0, 0.22, 12)
    ax.plot(noise_x, noise_y, "o", ms=2.5, color=C_GREY, alpha=0.45, zorder=2)
    # clean molecule on right
    molecule_icon(ax, cx + 0.72, cy, color=C_TEAL, scale=0.48)
    # arrow from noisy to clean
    ax.annotate("", xy=(cx + 0.45, cy), xytext=(cx - 0.45, cy),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=C_SLATE,
                                lw=1.2, mutation_scale=10, alpha=0.7),
                zorder=4)


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# LAYOUT — 5 zones on 12×6 canvas
# x positions (centers): 1.5 | 3.8 | 6.1 | 8.4 | 10.8
# Flow row at y=3.6; metrics row at y=1.35; headline at y=0.60
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FLOW_Y = 3.55
BOX_W  = 1.95
BOX_H  = 1.55

# ── Zone labels (top of figure) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
for xc, label in [(1.50, "INPUT"), (3.80, "RETRIEVAL"),
                  (6.10, "GENERATION"), (8.40, "OUTPUT")]:
    ax.text(xc, 5.55, label, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY, fontweight="bold",
            letterspacing=1.0, zorder=6)

# ── Zone 1: Protein Pocket ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
pocket_icon(ax, 1.50, FLOW_Y)
ax.text(1.50, FLOW_Y - 1.02, "Protein Pocket",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold",
        color=C_DARKGREY, zorder=6)
ax.text(1.50, FLOW_Y - 1.40, "binding-site conditioned",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY,
        style="italic", zorder=6)

# Arrow 1 → 2
flow_arrow(ax, 2.35, 2.82, FLOW_Y, color=C_GREY, lw=1.8)

# ── Zone 2: ChEMBL Retrieval ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Background box
rounded_box(ax, 3.80, FLOW_Y, BOX_W, BOX_H + 0.2,
            fc=C_MINT, ec=C_TEAL, lw=1.6, pad=0.18)
chembl_icon(ax, 3.80, FLOW_Y + 0.18)
ax.text(3.80, FLOW_Y - 0.62, "ChEMBL",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold",
        color=C_DARKGREY, zorder=6)
ax.text(3.80, FLOW_Y - 0.95, "ECFP4 top-k exemplars",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY,
        style="italic", zorder=6)

# Arrow 2 → 3
flow_arrow(ax, 4.78, 5.08, FLOW_Y, color=C_GREY, lw=1.8)
# "fed as exemplars" label on arrow
ax.text(4.93, FLOW_Y + 0.22, "exemplars",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7, color=C_GREY, zorder=6)

# ── Zone 3: Diffusion Decoder (the method) ───────────────────────────────────
rounded_box(ax, 6.10, FLOW_Y, BOX_W + 0.25, BOX_H + 0.2,
            fc=C_LAVENDER, ec=C_SLATE, lw=2.0, pad=0.18)
diffusion_icon(ax, 6.10, FLOW_Y + 0.15)
ax.text(6.10, FLOW_Y - 0.62, "RetroDiff",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10, fontweight="bold",
        color=C_SLATE, zorder=6)
ax.text(6.10, FLOW_Y - 0.97, "retrieval-augmented diffusion",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY,
        style="italic", zorder=6)

# Arrow 3 → 4
flow_arrow(ax, 7.20, 7.52, FLOW_Y, color=C_GREY, lw=1.8)

# ── Zone 4: Generated Molecules ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Three molecules at slightly different positions
offsets = [(-0.38, 0.38), (0.00, 0.00), (0.38, -0.28)]
scales  = [0.50, 0.60, 0.46]
alphas_bg = [0.7, 1.0, 0.6]
for (dx, dy), sc in zip(offsets, scales):
    molecule_icon(ax, 8.42 + dx, FLOW_Y + dy, color=C_TEAL, scale=sc)

ax.text(8.42, FLOW_Y - 0.90, "Generated Molecules",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold",
        color=C_DARKGREY, zorder=6)
ax.text(8.42, FLOW_Y - 1.24, "de novo drug candidates",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY,
        style="italic", zorder=6)

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# METRICS ROW — three badges
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
METRIC_Y = 1.52
METRIC_XS = [2.80, 6.00, 9.20]

# Thin separator line
ax.plot([1.0, 11.0], [METRIC_Y + 0.82, METRIC_Y + 0.82],
        color="#dee2e6", lw=0.8, zorder=1)

for xm, val, desc, col in [
    (METRIC_XS[0], "+18%",  "binding affinity gain\n(Vina docking score)", C_METRIC1),
    (METRIC_XS[1], "60%",   "data efficiency\n(matches baseline loss)", C_METRIC2),
    (METRIC_XS[2], "R²>0.9","scaling law confirmed\n(5 model sizes)",     C_METRIC3),
]:
    metric_badge(ax, xm, METRIC_Y, val, desc, col)

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# HEADLINE — embedded in figure (≤12 words)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
headline = "RetroDiff doubles hit rate over baseline diffusion at fixed compute."
ax.text(6.00, 0.58, headline,
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10.5,
        fontweight="bold", color=C_DARKGREY,
        zorder=7,
        bbox=dict(facecolor="#e8f4f1", edgecolor=C_TEAL,
                  boxstyle="round,pad=0.30", lw=1.2))

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# System name watermark — top-right
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ax.text(11.60, 5.65, "RetroDiff",
        ha="right", va="center", fontsize=11,
        fontweight="bold", color=C_TEAL,
        alpha=0.85, zorder=6)

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Save outputs
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig.savefig("figure.pdf", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight",
            facecolor=C_WHITE, backend="pdf")
fig.savefig("figure.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight",
            facecolor=C_WHITE)
fig.savefig("figure.svg", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight",
            facecolor=C_WHITE)

print("Saved: figure.pdf, figure.png, figure.svg")
plt.close(fig)
"}

    # ligand placeholder (small hexagon)
    n = 6
    hx = [cx + 0.18 * np.cos(2*np.pi*k/n) for k in range(n+1)]
    hy = [cy + 0.18 * np.sin(2*np.pi*k/n) + 0.02 for k in range(n+1)]
    ax.fill(hx, hy, color=C_TEAL, alpha=0.7, zorder=4)
    ax.plot(hx, hy, color=C_TEAL, lw=1.2, zorder=5)


def molecule_icon(ax, cx, cy, color=C_TEAL, scale=0.55):
    """Schematic 2D molecule (benzene-like ring + substituents)."""
    n = 6
    r = 0.28 * scale / 0.55
    # ring
    ring_x = [cx + r * np.cos(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6) for k in range(n+1)]
    ring_y = [cy + r * np.sin(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6) for k in range(n+1)]
    ax.fill(ring_x, ring_y, color=color, alpha=0.25, zorder=3)
    ax.plot(ring_x, ring_y, color=color, lw=1.8, zorder=4)
    # substituent sticks
    for k in [0, 2, 4]:
        bx = cx + r * np.cos(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6)
        by = cy + r * np.sin(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6)
        ex = bx + 0.22 * np.cos(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6)
        ey = by + 0.22 * np.sin(2*np.pi*k/n - np.pi/6)
        ax.plot([bx, ex], [by, ey], color=color, lw=1.5, zorder=4)
        ax.plot(ex, ey, "o", ms=4, color=color, alpha=0.8, zorder=5)


def chembl_icon(ax, cx, cy):
    """Mini database icon: stacked cylinder-like rects."""
    w, h = 0.7, 0.18
    gap = 0.05
    colors = [C_MINT, "#8ecfc0", "#6ab8a8"]
    for i, c in enumerate(colors):
        yy = cy + (1 - i) * (h + gap)
        box = FancyBboxPatch(
            (cx - w/2, yy - h/2), w, h,
            boxstyle="round,pad=0.04",
            facecolor=c, edgecolor=C_TEAL, linewidth=1.0, zorder=3
        )
        ax.add_patch(box)
    # top ellipse cap
    ellipse = mpatches.Ellipse((cx, cy + (h + gap) + h/2), w, 0.15,
                                facecolor="#6ab8a8", edgecolor=C_TEAL,
                                linewidth=1.0, zorder=4)
    ax.add_patch(ellipse)


def diffusion_icon(ax, cx, cy):
    """Schematic diffusion denoising: noisy → clean molecule trajectory."""
    # trajectory arc
    t = np.linspace(0, 1, 60)
    noise_amp = 0.22 * (1 - t)**1.4
    x_traj = cx - 0.8 + 1.6 * t
    y_traj = cy + noise_amp * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t * 3)
    ax.plot(x_traj, y_traj, color=C_SLATE, lw=2.0, alpha=0.7, zorder=3,
            solid_capstyle="round")
    # noise cloud on left
    rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
    noise_x = cx - 0.72 + rng.normal(0, 0.12, 12)
    noise_y = cy + rng.normal(0, 0.22, 12)
    ax.plot(noise_x, noise_y, "o", ms=2.5, color=C_GREY, alpha=0.45, zorder=2)
    # clean molecule on right
    molecule_icon(ax, cx + 0.72, cy, color=C_TEAL, scale=0.48)
    # arrow from noisy to clean
    ax.annotate("", xy=(cx + 0.45, cy), xytext=(cx - 0.45, cy),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=C_SLATE,
                                lw=1.2, mutation_scale=10, alpha=0.7),
                zorder=4)


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# LAYOUT — 5 zones on 12×6 canvas
# x positions (centers): 1.5 | 3.8 | 6.1 | 8.4 | 10.8
# Flow row at y=3.6; metrics row at y=1.35; headline at y=0.60
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FLOW_Y = 3.55
BOX_W  = 1.95
BOX_H  = 1.55

# ── Zone labels (top of figure) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
for xc, label in [(1.50, "INPUT"), (3.80, "RETRIEVAL"),
                  (6.10, "GENERATION"), (8.40, "OUTPUT")]:
    ax.text(xc, 5.55, label, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY, fontweight="bold",
            letterspacing=1.0, zorder=6)

# ── Zone 1: Protein Pocket ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
pocket_icon(ax, 1.50, FLOW_Y)
ax.text(1.50, FLOW_Y - 1.02, "Protein Pocket",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold",
        color=C_DARKGREY, zorder=6)
ax.text(1.50, FLOW_Y - 1.40, "binding-site conditioned",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY,
        style="italic", zorder=6)

# Arrow 1 → 2
flow_arrow(ax, 2.35, 2.82, FLOW_Y, color=C_GREY, lw=1.8)

# ── Zone 2: ChEMBL Retrieval ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Background box
rounded_box(ax, 3.80, FLOW_Y, BOX_W, BOX_H + 0.2,
            fc=C_MINT, ec=C_TEAL, lw=1.6, pad=0.18)
chembl_icon(ax, 3.80, FLOW_Y + 0.18)
ax.text(3.80, FLOW_Y - 0.62, "ChEMBL",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold",
        color=C_DARKGREY, zorder=6)
ax.text(3.80, FLOW_Y - 0.95, "ECFP4 top-k exemplars",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY,
        style="italic", zorder=6)

# Arrow 2 → 3
flow_arrow(ax, 4.78, 5.08, FLOW_Y, color=C_GREY, lw=1.8)
# "fed as exemplars" label on arrow
ax.text(4.93, FLOW_Y + 0.22, "exemplars",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7, color=C_GREY, zorder=6)

# ── Zone 3: Diffusion Decoder (the method) ───────────────────────────────────
rounded_box(ax, 6.10, FLOW_Y, BOX_W + 0.25, BOX_H + 0.2,
            fc=C_LAVENDER, ec=C_SLATE, lw=2.0, pad=0.18)
diffusion_icon(ax, 6.10, FLOW_Y + 0.15)
ax.text(6.10, FLOW_Y - 0.62, "RetroDiff",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10, fontweight="bold",
        color=C_SLATE, zorder=6)
ax.text(6.10, FLOW_Y - 0.97, "retrieval-augmented diffusion",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY,
        style="italic", zorder=6)

# Arrow 3 → 4
flow_arrow(ax, 7.20, 7.52, FLOW_Y, color=C_GREY, lw=1.8)

# ── Zone 4: Generated Molecules ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Three molecules at slightly different positions
offsets = [(-0.38, 0.38), (0.00, 0.00), (0.38, -0.28)]
scales  = [0.50, 0.60, 0.46]
alphas_bg = [0.7, 1.0, 0.6]
for (dx, dy), sc in zip(offsets, scales):
    molecule_icon(ax, 8.42 + dx, FLOW_Y + dy, color=C_TEAL, scale=sc)

ax.text(8.42, FLOW_Y - 0.90, "Generated Molecules",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold",

ax.text(8.42, FLOW_Y - 1.24, "de novo drug candidates",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5, color=C_GREY,
        style="italic", zorder=6)

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# METRICS ROW — three badges
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
METRIC_Y = 1.52
METRIC_XS = [2.80, 6.00, 9.20]

# Thin separator line
ax.plot([1.0, 11.0], [METRIC_Y + 0.82, METRIC_Y + 0.82],
        color="#dee2e6", lw=0.8, zorder=1)

for xm, val, desc, col in [

    metric_badge(ax, xm, METRIC_Y, val, desc, col)

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

headline = "RetroDiff doubles hit rate over baseline diffusion at fixed compute."
ax.text(6.00, 0.58, headline,
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10.5,
        fontweight="bold", color=C_DARKGREY,
        zorder=7,
        bbox=dict(facecolor="#e8f4f1", edgecolor=C_TEAL,
                  boxstyle="round,pad=0.30", lw=1.2))

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# System name watermark — top-right
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ax.text(11.60, 5.65, "RetroDiff",
        ha="right", va="center", fontsize=11,
        fontweight="bold", color=C_TEAL,
        alpha=0.85, zorder=6)

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Save outputs
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig.savefig("figure.pdf", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight",
            facecolor=C_WHITE, backend="pdf")
fig.savefig("figure.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight",
            facecolor=C_WHITE)
fig.savefig("figure.svg", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight",
            facecolor=C_WHITE)

print("Saved: figure.pdf, figure.png, figure.svg")
plt.close(fig)
